dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a breathing problem at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing. It started about 45 minutes ago when I began coughing.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your full name for me?
My name is John Smith.
What is the year today?
I'm not exactly sure, maybe 2020?
Can you tell me where you are right now?
I think I'm in my living room in [CITY], [STATE].
May we examine your chest and listen to your breathing to assess how you're doing?
I notice you are tripoding and using accessory muscles to breathe.
I can hear your breaths but they seem weak and poor. You're working very hard to breathe right now.
Your skin is pale, warm, and sweaty.
We're going to start a breathing treatment to help alleviate your distress.
Mr. Smith, we're going to check your oxygen level. You may feel a small clip on your finger.
Your oxygen level is at 68%, which is a bit low.
You're breathing very quickly, at about 28 breaths per minute.
I will take your pulse now. You may feel slight pressure.
Your heart is racing at a rate of 117 beats per minute.
We're going to take your blood pressure now. You won't feel this.
Your blood pressure reads 170 over 74.
We are going to use a CPAP unit to help with your breathing. You may feel some pressure here.
We're giving you a treatment with 5mg Albuterol and 0.5mg Atrovent to help open your airway.
We will now place some stickers to obtain your heart rhythm.
Your EKG is showing atrial fibrillation with a very fast heart rate.
You started coughing. Did you cough up anything?
No, it was just a dry cough.
When did this cough start?
It began about 45 minutes ago.
Mr. Smith, do you have a known medical history I should be aware of?
I have diabetes and need insulin. I also have atrial fibrillation.
Have you had any kidney-related issues or placed a shunt recently?
Yes, they put a shunt in my arm for dialysis. I'm not yet on dialysis, but I'll be starting in February.
Have you been diagnosed with COPD before?
No, this is different from any chronic lung issues I've had.
Your airway is clear, breathing is labored but equal with some poor air movement noted, and circulation is currently stable.
Mr. Smith, you are alert and oriented, knowing who you are, where you are, and the current situation.
Your skin is pale, warm, and very sweaty-this is known as diaphoresis. I don't see any rashes or abnormally dry or wet skin.
There are no visible injuries or deformities found in a quick scan of your body.
I feel movement protrusion under your sternum while you breathe-this is called a positive PMS.
Your oxygen level read 71%. It's slightly better, but still low.
Your breathing has slowed to 24 per minute, but you're still working very hard to breathe.
Your pulse is now at 121 beats per minute. It feels faster but is still bounding.
Your latest blood pressure is 136 over 86.
We repeated the heart scan and it still shows atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.
The 12-lead EKG confirms the rhythm. It appears there is also left ventricular hypertrophy present.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how difficult is your breathing for you?
I'd say about 8. I have a very hard time breathing, but no chest pain or other pain.
We'll take a quick blood sugar check now using a finger prick.
Your blood sugar is measuring at 0. It may be an empty reading or a contaminated sample.
Where do you want to be taken for treatment?
[COMPANY_NAME], please.
Mr. Smith, with your permission, we'll assist you in moving to a stair chair and then onto the ambulance stretcher.
Use your stronger leg to support your body as we assist you to a standing position.
Okay.
I'll adjust the chair for your comfort and safety so you can pivot over easily.
Now placing you on the stretcher and securing you for transport.
Let's gently move the stretcher into the ambulance with these handles.
Securing you with a harness for safety now.
It will take approximately 15 minutes to reach [CITY].
We've updated the hospital and highlighted your breathing difficulty and irregular heart rhythm as the priority.
[CITY] is aware and is expecting a 69-year-old male in respiratory distress with atrial fibrillation.
He is taking amlodipine, aspirin, clonidine, Zolpidem, and Hydralazine on a regular basis.
Currently using CPAP and a DuoNeb nebulizer, and we are monitoring him closely.
Vital signs are showing a slight improvement; oxygen remains low at 71%.
Positive pressure is noted beneath the sternum.
No IV was placed and he remains alert and oriented throughout the transport.
No drug allergies and the medications administered caused no adverse effects.
He reports no chest pain but has significant trouble breathing, rating it an 8 out of 10.
He has end-stage renal disease and is insulin-dependent for his diabetes.
That concludes the updated report.
Please sign the PCR once received, doctor.
Clear for transport. Closing the ambulance doors now.
We are en route to [COMPANY_NAME] and your patient is secured and stable.
Vital signs now show Pulse 121, Resp 24, BP 136/86, SpO2 71. CPAP and O2 are active.
EKG shows atrial fibrillation with rapid response on 4 lead for now.
Keeping your mask snug and maintaining vitals as a priority during transport.
Mr. Smith, confirming your latest oxygen reading.
Pulse is stable and heart rate is appropriate for transport.
Pulse check confirmed and oxygen remains consistent though still low.
Turning the direction slightly toward [CITY] now.
Current condition remains stable.
Lights and sirens remain on for prompt response.
Taking the direct route and staying aware of traffic.
Rejoining the main road now for quicker travel.
Giving the hospital a brief on current medical status.
We are now about 10 minutes out from [CITY].
Alerting ED to prepare for a male in distress with known cardiac and renal conditions.
Transport occurs with full ER awareness; RAM is ready for support if needed.
Paramedics are en route for hospital readiness.
ED, prepare for the arrival of a 69-year-old male in respiratory distress with chronic conditions.
RN has signed the PCR. Patient care has now been transferred.
Last vitals: 121, 24, 136/86, 71%. Vital signs are monitored closely.
Thanks for your cooperation, Mr. Smith. We'll keep you secure.
Comfortable and safe on this ride, Mr. Smith.
I'll monitor you all the way to offload.
All systems are ready and the patient remains stable.
Briefing [CITY] is complete and they are aware now.
No additional interventions needed. CPAP is still on.
On the look for any new changes or signs of distress during transport.
Adjusting CPAP settings as needed for your comfort and safety.
Keeping the ED aware of your condition at regular intervals.
Latest oxygen reading is consistent. CPAP working well.
No plans to remove CPAP unless you express discomfort or encounter machine issues.
Double-checking your heart rhythm. Atrial fib is stable with a rate near 120.
